questions.question: During routine surveillance at an NR facility you must determine whether the activity on a bench top is removable. The accepted method is to:
options: Hold a frisker probe directly against the bench — any reading above background proves the contamination is removable
Collect an air sample above the bench and count the filter
Wipe a smear pad across about 100 square centimeters of the surface and count the pad on a sensitive instrument
Measure the gamma dose rate at 30 centimeters from the bench
questions.explanation: A direct frisk reads total surface activity — fixed plus removable — and cannot tell you what transfers. Only a smear test answers that: the pad picks up whatever is loose over a standard ~100 cm² area and is counted on a sensitive instrument. Air sampling detects the airborne form, and a dose-rate reading measures the external field, not loose material.

questions.question: Dry sweeping is prohibited when cleaning up dry residue in a contamination area because a broom:
options: resuspends removable contamination into the air, converting it into the most dangerous, inhalable form
abrades the surface and converts removable contamination into fixed contamination
becomes neutron-activated and turns into a new radiation source
generates static charge that drives contamination deeper into the surface
questions.explanation: Airborne contamination is the most dangerous form because it can be inhaled, adding internal dose that continues until the material decays or is removed. Cleanup therefore works damp, from the outside edges inward, with HEPA-filtered vacuums for dry residue — a broom manufactures exactly the airborne hazard the cleanup exists to eliminate.

questions.question: Field gamma radiography is essentially contamination-free because the Ir-192 or Co-60 lives in a welded special-form capsule. Under 10 CFR 34.27, that capsule's integrity is proven by leak tests performed at intervals not exceeding:
options: three years, matching the typical source change-out cycle
six months
thirty days, at each dosimeter exchange
five years, at each exposure-device recertification
questions.explanation: Sealed sources are leak tested at intervals not exceeding six months precisely so the hazard stays external. NR work cannot make the same promise: activation is constant, transfer screens are radioactive by design, and a breached Cf-252 capsule scatters alpha emitters and fission products — which is why NR facilities run defined contamination zones and routine smear surveys.

questions.question: Which sequence is correct immediately before the shutter opens for a normal NR run?
options: Open the shutter from inside the cell, then exit quickly and secure the door behind you
Secure the door and open the shutter — the warning signal activates automatically once the area monitor detects the beam
Activate the warning signal first, then perform the search while the shutter drives open
Physically search the cell, leave last and secure the interlocked door, activate the warning signal, then open the shutter from the console — the only place beam control is permitted
questions.explanation: Search-and-secure proves the cell is empty before any beam exists, warnings precede the field rather than follow it, and beam control happens only at the console. Area monitors verify conditions independently — they are not the warning system, and nobody is ever inside a cell with the shutter under local control.

questions.question: After a run at a Cf-252 facility, the console indicates the source has returned to shielded storage, but your moderated neutron rem meter at the cell door still shows a significant field. You should:
options: treat it as a failed source transfer — do not enter; use the meter to establish a safe boundary, secure and post the area, and notify the RSO
enter briefly to visually confirm the source position, keeping your stay under two minutes
response-check the meter against its check source and enter if the second reading is lower
trust the console indication, since residual activation gamma commonly makes neutron meters read high
questions.explanation: Neutrons exist only while the source is exposed, so a neutron field at the door means the source is not in its shield — regardless of what the panel claims. Instruments outrank indicators, and recovering a stuck source or jammed shutter is planned work under the RSO's direction, never an improvised entry by the operator who found it.

questions.question: Unlike an accelerator-based neutron generator, a Cf-252 source can never truly be turned off because the capsule:
options: stays neutron-activated for several days after each exposure
continues fissioning only while the beam shutter is open
produces neutrons continuously by spontaneous fission (half-life about 2.6 years), so it only shuttles between shielded storage and exposure positions, emitting neutrons and gammas the entire time
is controlled by reactor operators under a separate operating license
questions.explanation: De-energizing an accelerator stops neutron production like switching off an X-ray machine. A radioisotope cannot be switched off: Cf-252 emits neutrons and gammas constantly, so for that facility 'off' means 'source proven to be in its shielded storage position' — and the procedures define exactly what that proof requires.

questions.question: Many procedures require relative humidity around 50 percent or higher whenever static-sensitive explosives are exposed. The humidity helps because:
options: moist air chemically desensitizes primary explosives in the device
moist air leaves a thin conductive film on surfaces that bleeds charge away nearly as fast as it is generated — the walk that produces 35,000 V in dry air may produce only about 1,500 V in humid air
humid air scatters thermal neutrons away from the explosive fill
high humidity lowers the voltage a person can perceive, so workers feel discharges sooner
questions.explanation: Humidity attacks charge retention, not explosive sensitivity: the conductive surface film drains static almost as fast as movement generates it. That is also why winter heating, air conditioning, and desert sites all raise the static threat — and why feeling nothing means nothing, since perception only begins around 3,000 volts.

questions.question: Setting up to radiograph an initiator, you notice a metal parts tray sitting on a rubber mat beside the workstation. The tray is a hazard because:
options: rubber outgasses vapors that corrode the initiator's bridgewire
the tray will short out your wrist strap's 1-megohm resistor
metal trays reflect thermal neutrons back into the explosive fill
insulated from ground, it is a floating conductor that can accumulate charge by contact or induction and deliver all of it in a single spark — bond it into the ground system or remove it
questions.explanation: An ungrounded conductor stores charge and waits to release it across the first gap it finds — possibly through the device. Every conductive item near an explosive device gets bonded or removed, and the rubber mat itself violates the rule that work surfaces near sensitive devices are grounded and conductive, never insulating.

questions.question: Under ATF storage rules in 27 CFR 555, explosives magazines must be inspected at least every:
options: 24 hours, with a complete piece-count inventory
30 days, by the licensee's responsible person
7 days — an inspection sufficient to detect unauthorized entry or attempted entry, not necessarily a full inventory
90 days, coinciding with the quarterly physical inventory
questions.explanation: The 7-day check is surveillance — locks, hasps, walls, signs of tampering — not a count. Paired with magazine records that capture every receipt and withdrawal, it is how a theft gets noticed fast, which matters because theft or loss must be reported to ATF and local law enforcement within 24 hours of discovery (27 CFR 555.30).

questions.question: A van leaves your facility carrying a single 45-kg case of Division 1.3C propellant-actuated devices. Placards on the vehicle are:
options: required — any quantity of Division 1.1, 1.2, or 1.3 explosives requires placarding
not required until the load reaches 454 kg (1,001 lb) aggregate gross weight
not required, because the devices hold an EX approval under 49 CFR 173.56
required only if compatibility group B detonators are also aboard
questions.explanation: The 454 kg (1,001 lb) threshold generally applies to Divisions 1.4 through 1.6; Divisions 1.1–1.3 are placarded at any quantity. An EX approval is the classification step that lets the item ship at all — it never waives hazard communication, packaging, shipping papers, or hazmat training.
